angle.headline: Why Operating Models Break When You Add More Countries
angle.thesis: Cross-border businesses fail not because they lack strategy, but because they try to scale a single-country operating model across multiple jurisdictions without redesigning how decisions get made and executed.
angle.targetPersona: US-based business owners expanding into LATAM markets or LATAM-based owners expanding into the US, particularly those managing operations across 2+ countries
angle.relevanceToAudience: The T-MEC review process (item 14) and the rising complexity of cross-border operations (items 16, 6) expose a critical pattern: businesses that succeed in one country often fail when they try to replicate that model across borders because they don't redesign their operating model to handle multiple regulatory environments, currencies, and decision-making frameworks simultaneously.

justification.angleChoice: This angle is superior to alternatives because it addresses a lived, concrete dysfunction that US-LATAM corridor operators face right now: the T-MEC review process (item 14) is forcing businesses to confront how fragile their cross-border operating models are. While items on AI adoption (9), succession planning, and workplace revolution exist in the bundle, none speak as directly to the ICP's immediate reality of managing operations across multiple jurisdictions. The Bunq expansion story (item 16) and Banco Plata's physical network deployment (item 6) demonstrate that even digital-first businesses must redesign their operating models for multi-country execution. The Fast Company piece (item 10) explicitly states that 65% of organizations are rewriting their business approaches every two years, and the top barrier is 'a disconnect between planning and execution' — which is precisely what happens when you scale a single-country model without redesigning the operating model. This angle avoids the competitive-signal trap of repeating surface-level trade policy coverage by focusing on the operational breakdown that trade complexity exposes.
justification.osPillarChoice: This is Operating Model OS, not Strategy OS or Technology OS, because the dysfunction is explicitly in execution, not direction or tooling. Item 14 shows that Mexican businesses understand the strategic importance of T-MEC (the CCE statement confirms this), but the challenge isn't strategic clarity — it's that their operating models were designed for a stable 16-year framework and now must adapt to annual reviews. Item 10 directly supports this: 'when asked about their top barrier to reinvention, 35% of CEOs said it's a disconnect between planning and execution.' Item 16 (Bunq) demonstrates this: they have a clear strategy (multi-currency accounts for cross-border users) but must wait up to 18 months for regulatory approval in Mexico, forcing them to redesign their operating model to serve European and Mexican customers simultaneously from a single platform. The bottleneck is not 'what to do' (Strategy OS) or 'what tools to use' (Technology OS) — it's 'how to execute decisions across multiple regulatory, currency, and operational contexts' (Operating Model OS). Item 9 reinforces this: organizations must 'redeploy talent quickly' and 'out-adapt their competitors' — both operational model challenges, not strategic ones.
justification.peopleAngleChoice: The challenge directly maps to the thesis because scaling across borders requires distributing decision-making authority, which creates a specific behavioral shift: the founder/owner must stop being the central approver for every cross-border decision. Kotter Step 5 (Empower) is the correct framework because the barrier is structural — centralized approval processes, single-country playbooks, and uniform policies all prevent local teams from acting on local conditions. The concrete action is removing these barriers by establishing clear decision rights and escalation protocols. This is evidenced in item 16, where Bunq must navigate both European and Mexican regulatory environments simultaneously, requiring them to empower local teams to execute within their jurisdictions. Item 4 (Ricardo Semler's management philosophy) supports this: 'replacing hierarchy with autonomy' is the behavioral shift required, and the framework activation is giving local operators the authority to execute within defined boundaries rather than requiring central approval for every decision.

peopleAngle.challenge: The leadership team must transition from making all cross-border decisions centrally to empowering local operators with clear decision rights and escalation protocols, which requires the founder to let go of approval authority on operational details they previously controlled.
peopleAngle.framework: Kotter Step 5: Empower — Remove the organizational barriers (centralized approval processes, single-country playbooks, uniform policies) that prevent local teams from executing within their jurisdictions while maintaining strategic alignment.

insightClaims.claim: Mexico's Secretary of Economy announced that the T-MEC will not be extended to 2042 and will instead be subject to annual reviews through 2036
According to the US Bureau of Economic Analysis, Mexico maintained its position as the top supplier to the United States with a 16.9% share of merchandise imports
Bunq is waiting up to 18 months for regulatory approval from Mexico's CNBV before it can offer multi-currency accounts that manage Mexican and European accounts simultaneously from one platform
A survey of 700+ C-suite leaders found that 35% of CEOs identified the disconnect between planning and execution as their top barrier to reinvention

Banco Plata is deploying 50 intelligent ATMs initially and plans to reach 300 units nationwide, with the first phase covering eight cities including Mexico City, Monterrey, and Guadalajara between June and August 2026

fieldReportAnchor.company: Bunq (Dutch neobank expanding to Mexico with multi-currency accounts)

enContent.subject: Your team isn't slow. Your approval loop is.
enContent.preheader: Your operating model rewards escalation. Rewrite the loop.
subjectOptions: Your team isn't slow. Your approval loop is.
The cross-border decisions still landing in your inbox
USMCA is reopening. Your approval chain isn't ready.

sections.heading: The Signal
sections.body:
*This week: The bottleneck across every pillar is the same. Authority has never been mapped to where the work actually happens.*

- **Strategy:** [Monomoy completed its $1.3 billion acquisition of Jiffy Lube from Shell](https://middlemarketgrowth.org/pe-taco-bell-yumearth-food-beverage/), pulling a decentralized service network out of a corporate parent. **A conglomerate that owns everything owns nothing well.**
- **Operating Models:** [Fast Company reports](https://www.fastcompany.com/91567522/its-time-to-rethink-your-operating-model) 93% of executives say the operating model must be challenged every five years, and 65% now rewrite theirs every two. **The pace of adaptation is the operating model.**
- **Technology:** [Supply Chain Dive documents](https://www.supplychaindive.com/news/digital-twins-software-maturity-lead-manufacturing-automation-trends/824288/) simulation software and digital twins becoming more cost-effective on the factory floor. **A simulation is only as smart as the decisions it can authorize.**
- **Human Capital:** [HR Executive reports](https://hrexecutive.com/the-death-of-the-megacorporation-may-be-a-bigger-story-than-layoffs/) HiBob's CEO predicts winners will redeploy talent faster than they build the largest workforces. **Headcount is not the moat. Redeployment speed is.**

sections.heading: The Apertura
sections.body:
===OPTION_A:statistic===
[Fast Company reports](https://www.fastcompany.com/91567522/its-time-to-rethink-your-operating-model) that 65% of organizations now rewrite their operating model every two years or sooner. Ninety-three percent of executives agree the model must be challenged at least every five. And yet, 35% of CEOs name the disconnect between planning and execution as their top barrier to reinvention. This is not a planning problem. Planning happens. Execution stalls because the operating model built for last year's scale still routes every decision through the founder. The redesign lives on the whiteboard. The approval loop has not changed.
===OPTION_B:provocation===
The operating model is not the org chart. It is the set of decisions the org chart implies but never states. [Fast Company found](https://www.fastcompany.com/91567522/its-time-to-rethink-your-operating-model) that 35% of CEOs cite the gap between planning and execution as their top barrier to reinvention. That gap has a specific location. It lives between a manager who knows a call is theirs to make and a founder who never wrote that boundary down. The most consequential lines in any operating model are the ones nobody has drawn yet.
===OPTION_C:pattern===
Three signals this week point at the same tension. [Fast Company](https://www.fastcompany.com/91567522/its-time-to-rethink-your-operating-model) reports 65% of organizations now rewrite their operating model every two years. [Chief Executive](https://chiefexecutive.net/how-to-lead-a-workplace-revolution-without-losing-your-board-probably/) revisits Ricardo Semler's argument that autonomy, not hierarchy, is the durable form. [HR Executive](https://hrexecutive.com/the-death-of-the-megacorporation-may-be-a-bigger-story-than-layoffs/) predicts the winners of the next decade will redeploy talent faster than they hire it. Different articles. One argument underneath. The centralized approval structures that got mid-market companies to their first ceiling are the same structures now keeping them from the next one.

sections.heading: The Insight
sections.body:
You built your business by holding every cross-border decision. That decision volume is now choking growth.

The pattern is familiar. An operator in Monterrey waits three days for a vendor approval. A team lead in Bogotá pauses a hire while the founder's inbox fills. This is not laziness or timidity. This is the operating model built when the company was smaller than it is today.

Here is the mechanism. You centralized authority because the corridor was complex and the stakes were personal. That worked at $5M. It breaks at $30M. The system you built to protect the business is now the ceiling on the business.

The instinct is to hire another executive or install a workflow tool. Neither solves the underlying problem. [Fast Company found](https://www.fastcompany.com/91567522/its-time-to-rethink-your-operating-model) that 35% of CEOs cite the disconnect between planning and execution as their top barrier to reinvention. That gap is not a communication problem. It is a decision-rights problem.

**Jurisdictional Decision Architecture**

The Operating Model OS treats cross-border authority as a mapping problem, not a delegation problem. You are not delegating. You are drawing jurisdictional lines.

Three layers. Layer one: decisions that stay with you regardless of geography, such as capital allocation above a threshold, strategic pivots, and senior hires in any country. Layer two: decisions the local operator makes and reports on, such as vendor selection within a budget, hiring below a level, and customer terms within a range. Layer three: decisions the local operator makes without reporting, such as scheduling, local supplier issues, and small operational calls.

The hardest work is layer two. Layer one is easy to name. Layer three is easy to release. In our experience, layer two is where founders hoard without realizing it. Letting go here requires trust. Trust that a local operator, in a different culture and regulatory context, can make a call the founder would have made differently. That trust does not appear from a speech. It appears from a written boundary, and the discipline of holding to it when a decision comes back different than expected.

There is a stage in every change process where the leadership work is not to inspire but to remove barriers. This is that stage. The barrier here is not policy. The barrier is the founder's own approval habit.

**This week**

Pull the last twenty decisions that reached you from operators outside your home country. For each, ask: which layer? If it should have lived in layer two or three, write down whose call it actually is. Share the list by Friday. The exercise takes one hour. The clarity it creates lasts a year.

sections.heading: The Field Report
sections.body:
[Ford's CEO Jim Farley told CNBC](https://www.cnbc.com/2026/07/02/ford-jim-farley-usmca.html) that the automaker assembled over 2 million vehicles in the United States last year. That is more than any other manufacturer, including 311,000 units for export. He is pushing for a level playing field with Toyota and GM on imports as USMCA trade talks reopen.

For the mid-market operator running a US-LATAM corridor business, the reading of this shift is direct. Trade architecture is being rewritten while most operators still route every cross-border decision through the founder's inbox. Our view is that when the rules change, the businesses that adapt are the ones whose local teams already hold authority to move. Waiting for the founder to approve a supplier switch or a routing change is a cost the corridor will not absorb twice.

sections.heading: The Tool
sections.body: **Tango.** A browser extension that captures a process step-by-step just by watching you do it once, turning it into a shareable SOP with screenshots. It matters because a decision rights map is only useful when the underlying procedures are documented well enough for someone else to actually execute them without asking. Find it at [tango.us](https://www.tango.us).

sections.heading: The Compass
sections.body: **Watch for this week:** Count how many cross-border operational decisions reach your inbox by Friday. Not strategic decisions. Operational ones. A vendor question, a scheduling call, a small hire. Every decision on that list is one your local operator could not act on without you. That count is the shape of your Operating Model OS today. It is also the first number to change.

sections.heading: The Door
sections.body:
If something in this issue landed, reply - I read every response.

If this is useful to someone in your network, forward it - it takes ten seconds and it's the highest compliment.

When you're ready to work together directly, here is how we start: [link]

esContent.subject: Su equipo no es lento. El circuito de aprobaciones sí.
esContent.preheader: Su modelo operativo premia el escalamiento. Rediseñe el circuito.
esContent.thesis: Las empresas del corredor no fracasan por falta de estrategia. Fracasan cuando intentan escalar un modelo operativo de un solo país a varias jurisdicciones sin rediseñar dónde se toman y se ejecutan las decisiones.
esContent.shareableSentence: El sistema que armó para proteger el negocio se convirtió en el techo del negocio.

sections.heading: LA SEÑAL
sections.body:
*Esta semana: el cuello de botella se repite en cada pilar. La autoridad nunca quedó donde ocurre el trabajo.*

- **Estrategia:** [Monomoy cerró la compra de Jiffy Lube a Shell](https://middlemarketgrowth.org/pe-taco-bell-yumearth-food-beverage/) por unos 1,300 mdd, sacando una red descentralizada de servicio del paraguas corporativo. **Un conglomerado que lo controla todo no controla nada bien.** [Leer en Middle Market Growth (ACG) ->](https://middlemarketgrowth.org/pe-taco-bell-yumearth-food-beverage/)
- **Modelos Operativos:** [Fast Company reporta](https://www.fastcompany.com/91567522/its-time-to-rethink-your-operating-model) que 93% de los ejecutivos coincide en que el modelo operativo debe cuestionarse cada cinco años, y 65% ya lo reescribe cada dos. **El ritmo de adaptación es el modelo operativo.** [Leer en Fast Company ->](https://www.fastcompany.com/91567522/its-time-to-rethink-your-operating-model)
- **Tecnología:** [Supply Chain Dive documenta](https://www.supplychaindive.com/news/digital-twins-software-maturity-lead-manufacturing-automation-trends/824288/) que el software de simulación y los gemelos digitales son cada vez más rentables en piso de planta. **Una simulación vale lo que valen las decisiones que puede autorizar.** [Leer en Supply Chain Dive ->](https://www.supplychaindive.com/news/digital-twins-software-maturity-lead-manufacturing-automation-trends/824288/)
- **Capital Humano:** [HR Executive reporta](https://hrexecutive.com/the-death-of-the-megacorporation-may-be-a-bigger-story-than-layoffs/) que el CEO de HiBob prevé que los ganadores redesplegarán talento más rápido, en vez de armar las plantillas más grandes. **La plantilla no es el foso. La velocidad de redespliegue sí.** [Leer en HR Executive ->](https://hrexecutive.com/the-death-of-the-megacorporation-may-be-a-bigger-story-than-layoffs/)

sections.heading: LA APERTURA
sections.body: [Fast Company informa](https://www.fastcompany.com/91567522/its-time-to-rethink-your-operating-model) que 65% de las organizaciones reescribe su modelo operativo cada dos años, y que 93% de los ejecutivos considera que debe cuestionarse al menos cada cinco. No obstante, 35% de los CEOs señala la desconexión entre planeación y ejecución como su principal freno para reinventarse. No es un problema de planeación. La planeación ocurre. Lo que se traba es la ejecución: el modelo operativo diseñado para la escala del año pasado sigue enrutando cada decisión por el fundador. El rediseño vive en el pizarrón. El circuito de aprobaciones no cambió.

sections.heading: EL INSIGHT
sections.body:
Usted construyó esta empresa porque retuvo cada decisión que cruzaba una frontera. Ese volumen de decisiones ahora asfixia el crecimiento.

El patrón es conocido. Un operador en Monterrey espera tres días la autorización de un proveedor. Una líder de equipo en Bogotá pausa una contratación mientras la bandeja del fundador se llena. No es pereza ni timidez. Es el modelo operativo que se diseñó cuando la empresa era más pequeña de lo que es hoy.

Aquí está el mecanismo. Usted centralizó la autoridad porque el corredor es complejo y las consecuencias son personales. Funcionó a los 5 millones de dólares. Se rompe a los 30. El sistema que armó para proteger el negocio terminó siendo el techo del negocio.

La reacción típica es contratar a otro ejecutivo o instalar una herramienta de flujo de trabajo. Ninguna resuelve la raíz. [Fast Company encontró](https://www.fastcompany.com/91567522/its-time-to-rethink-your-operating-model) que 35% de los CEOs señala la desconexión entre planeación y ejecución como su principal barrera para reinventarse. Esa brecha no es un problema de comunicación. Es un problema de derechos de decisión.

**Arquitectura jurisdiccional de decisiones**

El Operating Model OS trata la autoridad transfronteriza como un problema de trazado, no de delegación. No se trata de delegar. Se trata de dibujar líneas jurisdiccionales.

Tres capas. La primera: decisiones que permanecen con usted sin importar la geografía, como asignación de capital por encima de cierto umbral, pivotes estratégicos y contrataciones sénior en cualquier país. La segunda: decisiones que el operador local toma y reporta, como selección de proveedores dentro del presupuesto, contrataciones por debajo de cierto nivel y términos con clientes dentro de un rango. La tercera: decisiones que el operador local toma sin necesidad de reportar, como agenda, incidencias con proveedores locales y llamadas operativas menores.

El trabajo duro está en la segunda capa. La primera es fácil de nombrar. La tercera es fácil de soltar. En nuestra experiencia, la segunda es donde el fundador acumula sin advertirlo. Soltarla exige confianza. Confianza en que un operador local, en otra cultura y otro marco regulatorio, decida de una forma distinta a la que el fundador habría elegido. Esa confianza no aparece en un discurso. Aparece con una frontera escrita, y con la disciplina de sostenerla cuando la decisión regresa distinta a la esperada.

Hay una etapa en todo proceso de cambio donde el trabajo del liderazgo no es inspirar, sino remover obstáculos. Es esta. El obstáculo aquí no es una política. Es el hábito de aprobación del fundador.

**Esta semana**

Revise las últimas veinte decisiones que le llegaron de operadores fuera de su país. Para cada una, pregúntese: ¿qué capa? Si debió vivir en la segunda o tercera, escriba de quién era realmente la llamada. Comparta la lista antes del viernes. El ejercicio toma una hora. La claridad que genera dura un año.

sections.heading: EL REPORTE DE CAMPO
sections.body:
Sección: spotlight

[El Consejo Coordinador Empresarial reaccionó con cautela](https://elceo.com/negocios/cautela-optimismo-y-desacuerdos-las-posturas-del-empresariado-mexicano-sobre-el-t-mec/) ante el anuncio de la Secretaría de Economía: el T-MEC no se extenderá de forma automática hasta 2042, sino que quedará sujeto a revisiones anuales, con vigencia hasta 2036. José Medina Mora, presidente del CCE, subrayó que México se mantiene como principal proveedor de Estados Unidos con 16.9% de las importaciones. La tercera ronda bilateral arranca la semana del 20 de julio en la Ciudad de México.

Para el dueño de una empresa mediana en el corredor, la lectura es directa. La arquitectura comercial se reescribe con revisiones anuales, no con horizontes de dos décadas. Nuestra postura: cuando las reglas cambian con esa frecuencia, sobrevive quien ya tiene autoridad distribuida en la operación local. Esperar a que el fundador apruebe un cambio de proveedor o una ruta alterna es un costo que el corredor no absorberá dos veces.

sections.heading: LA HERRAMIENTA
sections.body: **Tango.** Una extensión de navegador que captura un proceso paso a paso con solo observarlo una vez, y lo convierte en un procedimiento compartible con capturas de pantalla. Vale la pena porque un mapa de derechos de decisión solo funciona cuando los procedimientos que lo sostienen están documentados con suficiente detalle para que otra persona los ejecute sin necesidad de preguntar. Está en [tango.us](https://www.tango.us).

sections.heading: LA BRÚJULA
sections.body: **A vigilar esta semana:** cuente cuántas decisiones operativas transfronterizas llegan a su bandeja antes del viernes. No las estratégicas. Las operativas. Una duda de proveedor, un ajuste de agenda, una contratación menor. Cada decisión en esa lista es una que su operador local no pudo resolver sin usted. Ese número refleja el estado actual de su Operating Model OS. Es también el primer número que debe cambiar.

sections.heading: LA PUERTA
sections.body:
Si algo de este número resonó contigo, respóndeme - leo cada mensaje.
Si le es útil a alguien en tu red, reenvíalo - es el mayor cumplido.
Cuando estés listo para trabajar juntos directamente, así es como empezamos: [link]

assets.prompt: A precise pencil sketch illustration showing an organizational flowchart fragmenting into multiple parallel streams, each representing a different country jurisdiction. The central approval node—depicted as an overloaded architectural keystone—cracks under pressure while decision pathways queue and tangle. In contrast, a redesigned structure on the right shows clean, independent decision columns with clear horizontal lines marking authority thresholds. Use charcoal graphite for the congested left side, white space for breathing room, and a single orange line highlighting the new jurisdictional boundary that enables local execution. The composition suggests architectural blueprints being redrawn, with the headline "Your team isn't slow. Your approval loop is." positioned as an editorial serif statement. Contemporary office environment, white background, large negative space, one central metaphor.

altText.en: Abstract illustration showing interconnected nodes in different colors, with some paths blocked and others flowing freely through a network structure
altText.es: Ilustración abstracta de nodos interconectados en distintos colores, con algunas rutas bloqueadas y otras fluyendo libremente en una estructura de red

caption.en: Your approval loop becomes the bottleneck the moment you add a second country.
caption.es: Tu circuito de aprobaciones se convierte en cuello de botella en cuanto sumas un segundo país.
